item.content:encoded: Congrats on the +100! This one deserves it, it's a really interesting take on depressed Greens, and approaches the suicide theme with tact and respect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good questions. They do perceive Andrew getting older with them, appearing to age normally. The answer to your second question might be considered some kind of spoilers, so if I'm putting it behind a thing to keep some mystery.

+ Mystery
- Reveal

As each of the fourteen people die, one of the threads holding the body will be cut. When there are no more threads the body in the pocket dimension will fall into the water below, the pocket dimension will close up, and there will be no more anomaly because there will no longer be a need for one.

I always thought there would be a lot of little weirdnesses in the SCP universe, the implication that there are at least some things the Foundation doesn't catch and that aren't really important to the universe at large. This is just a story that played out before the Foundation could get to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A few questions. As the people on the list get older, do they also perceive Andrew getting older with them as well? And when all those people die, what happens to Andrew?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The one thing keeping me from liking this is that I can't see why Andrew wanted to kill himself. Sure, he's described as being shy and reclusive, but he seems to have a decent-sized circle of friends and the apparent ability to fold reality and while typing this I just remembered that one photograph of Kurt Cobain eating a whole pizza with a big goofy grin on his face so never mind. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall I have a lot of questions about this SCP still after reading it, but you've done a good job with putting the focus on the hallucination itself rather than the anomaly of the little alt-universe "bubble" he made. Unfortunately, I also still have a lot of questions about the bubble (like the pool of water at the bottom that doesn't appear to have a significance, unless I'm missing something big here) and so it's still kind of distractingly confusing.

However, you've also got my attention with Andrew's suicide, and the fact that he went through with it because he knew he could keep the people in his life from suffering because of it. Whether it was intentional or not, I can appreciate the fact that this article touches on the issue of current suicide prevention that focuses entirely on how other people are affected by someone's suicide, and not focusing on the suicidal person at all.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I'm coming into this late (because an SCP blog posted fanart), but I just want to say as someone who's been suicidal this is one of my favorites. "I built a net for my friends" would have maybe worked best as either the last line, something like something one of the friends said, or the title, because it really hits. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liked the idea and the execution. Only suggestion would be to make the first note more personalized, perhaps with Andrew proposing several locations to use (under the kitchen sink? west window? and so on) and/or "detailing" a little bit of the process used (this one could be simply be put in the notes introduction, if you have not thought about one).
Otherwise, +1 from moi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me sad. Most SCPs on here make me entertained, or amused, or creeped out, but this is the first one that's ever actually legitimately lowered my mood. The detail in him not wanting his family and friends to miss him, hiding his depression, it's just too real. I guess that's the hallmark of an effective story, though. It made me feel a thing. Take your +1, you monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A good read, and I'm happy I took the time to read the whole thing. I do think that note #1 is completely unnecessary, even with the in-universe explanation that he maybe wrote it only to put things off for a bit more, but it honestly doesn't need to be included and actually killed the overall tone, for me. The simple list as the only recovered document is more effective, and all the stuff about folding, the room's corner, "don't make them worry about you"…all of that is covered elsewhere, and better, since it's not laid out so directly. Still, have a +1!

edit: maaaaaybe the list isn't entirely legible? I don't know how that could help or hinder the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I've added something that should clarify that, thanks for pointing it out.

2) I consider the two terms interchangeable but what I was trying to get across was that this is a kind of bubble universe rather than a true alternate dimension, I guess. It's just this one space, there's nothing else around it.

3) Ah, I can't believe I missed that, thanks for pointing it out. I'm going to be revamping the notes so that might not be applicable down the line, but I'll keep it in mind.

Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of a guy who somehow meta-folded himself out of reality and still shows up for some reason (I like it even more given the spoiler comment), but I am filled with questions.

1) His clothes were common for where in 2005? Fashion can differ wildly with location and culture.

2) Why "extrauniversal"? "Extradimensional" is the more common term, so is the usage here meant to evoke a different meaning? If so, what exactly does it suggest?

3) If they had document #2 and knew the names corresponded to the 2784 instances, how did the Foundation not know there were supposed to be 14 2784's, including Andrew's parents?

I'll second the suggestion to drop the first addendum, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this really touching. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The realism of the suicide isn't what had bothered me, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some people write stuff down. I mean, you can have a little fifth-grader with a notebook and a huge stack of paper, or an adult who didn't even bother to leave a note before blowing his brains out. People just go about their suicides differently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty solid overall, but I think you could do without the first note, since any information there is covered in the interview, and it's a little bit too straightforward for the piece — I imagine anyone who's been suicidal, or hung around with suicidal people, would twig onto what this is about pretty quickly once it was implied that Andrew was capable of doing it, so doing it more than once isn't necessary.

Also, I just like interviews a lot better than recovered documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like minmin, I've got some quibbles, but it hit me hard.

I'm in agreement with the exposition being a little on-the-nose. In addition, I thought the "tone" could be a little tighter: worst crit ever, I know, and I can't even back it up with an example that I find particularly egregious — it's more like slight ambiguities and stilted phraseologies that occasionally break flow. e.g. it's a bit comma-light in places.

Another point related to the "tone" complaint: I had to get to the end of the -A paragraph to figure out how the sub-des related to the anomaly, which made me a little confused for a while. (in a "huh, what?" way rather than an "ooh, I wonder what" way.) Personally, I would have opened with telling us the location of where it was found — which, happily, would have tied nicely into the end of the preceding paragraph.

But, on balance, I'm upvoting. The emotional punch of realising what Andrew did was surprising and well-delivered, and more than offsets any structural flaws. Gave me a big bruise on the ol' feelings. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I view it as a kind of psyching himself up/procrastinating before going through with it, just writing these notes that detail his plan to himself before he actually does it.

At the end of the day I'd rather the reader left the article knowing what happened. I'm okay with asking for a suspension of disbelief in order to get the story across, though it seems I could stand to be a little more subtle about it, so that's something to work on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, thank you! That's some great feedback!

The suicide is the one thing I didn't want to be ambiguous here, so I guess I ended up focusing more on mechanics when I should have been showing intent. That gives me a better idea of what to tweak to make this one better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get why the guy would just leave a couple of notes like that lying around. It is awfully convenient to the plot, and really nothing more than that. I mean, why even write down the names of those he affects with this? Part of the ritual? If he really cared enough about these people to spare them the grief, wouldn't he be able to keep a mental note of who they were?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted when I read the note and got it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exposition leaning on the heavy-handed, but I don't think I can downvote this in good spirit. It made me feel… things.

I'm just going to wrap myself up and go to sleep now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's a lot that's good about this, but it doesn't quite gel for me. The fact that you're really subtle about the suicide is appropriate and realistic, but it also muddles the story and leaves a lot of room for confusion.

Also, overall, it's very… simplistic. You've compressed the story down into a very neat package, but it that leaves me less than satisfied. A person committed suicide and left an echo of himself. We know how that happened because he spelled it out in a note, and he was able to do it because he had anomalous powers exactly related to how he was found… as he explained… in the note.

I guess what I'm getting at here is that the only thing ambiguous about this entire article is the fact that it was a suicide. Everything else is spelled out, and I think that choice hurts both sides of the equation.

-1

Edit: I should say that you also include a lot of hesitation on the Foundation's part about whether the apparition or body are, in fact Andrew's, but again, this is a mystery that doesn't enhance your story. There's never any other alternative and already a very clear explanation, so it's a lot of hemming and hawing over nothing. Is it a realistic amount of skepticism for the Foundation to hold? Yes, but it's not interesting to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, when I was reading this in chat as a draft, I had originally thought that Andrew was doing all the spooky voodoo string stuff as a means of achieving a form of immorality, and that it was an interesting means of going about that. Then the realization about what the actual intent of the strings were hit me, and it felt like a punch to the gut.

This is a well written SCP, and I think you do the the subject matter justice.

+1. Good show Lex

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rarely get much time to look at skips (I wish there were more hours in the day) but I caught this one.

I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really well written, interesting concept, executed perfectly in my opinion. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Behold: a skip.

Big thanks to Conwell, Quin, Rimple, Zyn, ARD, shaggydredlocks, and NineVolt for feedback.

Confused? Here's…

A SPOILER
or maybe not

It's about suicide. A better title might have been "I have made a net for my friends."

Edit 10/26/16: Added more flavor to the paragraph explaining the two documents so it's not just "the Foundation found some notes." Credit goes to Rimple for the idea.
